featured_videos.editorial_title: when the kids start asking for steak  ￼#carnivore #food #carnivorediet #steak
featured_videos.commentary: Okay, so the comments are divided on doneness levels, but honestly that's the least important thing happening here. What's actually wild is watching kids genuinely prefer steak over the typical kid food trap, and the community's here for it. This is what happens when you normalize real food at the dinner table instead of chicken nuggets and pasta. Props to Monique for raising kids who know what good tastes like.

featured_videos.editorial_title: I eat one meal a day with these 5 foods ✅ #omad #carnivore #health #nutrition #healthyfood #fasting
featured_videos.commentary: The comment section is having a field day with the evolution here, and honestly, they've got a point. What's interesting isn't the contradiction though, it's what it actually reveals. OMAD works best when it works for *you*, and that looks different for different people. Some people thrive on pure meat, some add in the foods that make their digestion happy and their energy stable. There's no shame in figuring out what your body actually needs versus what diet dogma says you should want. The real win is finding the one-meal rhythm that you can actually stick to without feeling deprived or wired at midnight.

The best part of this video isn't even the ribeye. it's watching people in the comments defend Cam against the usual "he's fake" accusations. Look, if you've been following carnivore content long enough, you know the community can be brutal, but there's something refreshing about someone just showing up consistently to cook good meat and support local butchers. That's the real flex. The ribeye itself is gorgeous, and yeah, the butcher route beats supermarket every time when it comes to quality and knowing where your food actually comes from.

featured_videos.editorial_title: Steak and eggs for the win #carnivore #steak #meat #food #carnivorediet
featured_videos.commentary: I see the comment section is basically divided between "rest your steak, always" and "honestly, I've been doing it this way and it works fine." Here's the thing. both camps are right, but for different reasons. Resting does help with juice retention and even cooking, but if you're eating steak and eggs for satiation and metabolic health, you're getting the nutrients either way. The real win here is consistency and actually cooking meat regularly instead of overthinking the process into paralysis. If Monique's method gets someone cooking carnivore meals at home instead of ordering takeout, that's a bigger win than debating resting time in the comments.
